coredata.dc:title: Interfacial Bi-O-C bonds and rich oxygen vacancies synergistically endow carbon quantum dot/Bi2MoO6 with prominent photocatalytic CO2 reduction into CO

coredata.dc:description: Developing the photocatalysts with the low charge-transfer resistance and fast charge kinetics is crucial to photocatalytic CO2 reduction into hydrocarbon fuels. Herein, we depict the interfacial Bi-O-C bond-bridged carbon quantum dot (CD)/oxygen vacancy-rich Bi2MoO6 (VOR-BMO) Ohmic junction photocatalysts. They were constructed by in-situ anchoring carbon quantum dots (CDs) onto the VOR-BMO hierarchical petal-like structures (HPs), which are self-assembled from numerous nanosheet subunits. Experimental analysis together with density functional theory (DFT) calculations indicate that the Bi-O-C bonds can provide the atomic-level interfacial channels, and introducing rich oxygen vacancies (VOs) into the BMO HPs can induce the enhanced built-in electric field (IEF) of the Ohmic heterojunction formed between VOR-BMO HPs and CDs, synergistically contributing the eminent charge transfer kinetics, so as to sharply boost the separation and transfer of the photoinduced electrons of VOR-BMO HPs into CDs with a near-zero resistance; Besides, the CD/VOR-BMO heterostructures can not only change the endoergic rate-determining step of VO-poor (VOP)-BMO HPs (i.e., CO* desorption to form CO) to an exoergic reaction process, thus promoting the CO* desorption from the photocatalyst surface, but also decrease the overall activation energy barrier. Consequently, in the presence of H2O vapor with no sacrificial agent, the optimum photocatalyst (CD/VOR-BMO-1.2) exhibits the fantabulous performances of CO2 photoreduction into CO with the production rate and selectivity of 60.00 μmol·g−1·h−1 and near 100 %, respectively. Such performances are comparable with the most recently reported photocatalysts for CO2 conversion. The study offers a valid strategy that harnesses the synergistic effect of the interfacial chemical bonds and Vo-induced enhanced IEF to design the high-efficiency photocatalysts for both energy and environmental applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of BMO HPs and CD/BMO hybrids.
(a, b) FE-SEM, (c) TEM, (d) HRTEM image of CD/VOR-BMO-1.2; (e) EDX elemental mappings of CD/VOR-BMO-1.2.

(a) EPR spectra and (d) FT-IR spectra of VOP-BMO HPs, VOR-BMO HPs, CD/VOP-BMO −1.2 and CD/VOR-BMO-1.2; (b) The DOS calculations of VOR-BMO structure; (c) The optimized configurations of CD/VOR-BMO heterojunction; The XPS high-resolved spectra of (e) Bi 4 f, (f) Mo 4 f, (g) O 1 s, (h) C 1 s for VOP-BMO HPs, VOR-BMO HPs and CD/VOR-BMO-1.2; (i) Side view of the charge density difference over the CD/VOR-BMO heterojunction (The yellow and cyan areas represent accumulation and depletion of electrons, respectively).

(a) Time evolutions of CO and (b) average gas production rates over different catalysts in which error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent measurements using fresh sample for each measurement; (c) recycling test of photocatalytic CO production and (d) MS spectra of 13CO production of 13CO2 photoreduction over CD/VOR-BMO-1.2.
(a) Energy band diagrams of BMO HPs and CDs after contact; Comparison of the photoinduced charge-carrier migration on (b) CD/VOP-BMO and (c) CD/VOR-BMO upon light irradiation.
High-resolution in situ XPS spectra for (a) Bi 4 f, (b) Mo 3d, (c) O 1 s and (d) C 1 s of CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 either in dark or under (visible light) irradiation.

Height images of (a) CD/VOR-BMO-1.2, (b) CD/VOP-BMO-1.2; The SPV images in (a′), (b′) are differential images between potential images under light and in the dark. Surface photovoltage change by subtracting the potential under dark conditions from that under illumination (SPV, ΔCPD =CPD light − CPD dark) over (c) CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 and (d) CD/VOP-BMO-1.2; (e) Photocurrent responses, (f) Nyquist plots and (g) TRPL decay traces of the pre-obtained samples.

Stable adsorption configurations for (a, b) CO2 and (c, d) H2O over BMO (010) with the VO and CDs of CD/VOR-BMO. The corresponding adsorption energies are listed below each structure. The purple, gray, red, brown and white spheres stand for Bi, Mo, O, C, and H atoms, respectively.

(a) CO2-TPD curve and (b) CO-TPD curves for the pre-obtained samples; (c) In situ DRIFT profiles for CO2 photoreduction over CD/VOP-BMO-1.2; (d) Calculated Gibbs free energy profile of CO2 reduction into CO catalyzed by VOP-BMO HPS and CD/VOR-BMO-1.2.
Proposed schematic diagram of the enhanced photocatalytic mechanism of the reduction of CO2 over CD/VOR-BMO-1.2.
Schematic illustration of preparation for BMO HPs and CD/BMO hybrids.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing the photocatalysts with the low charge-transfer resistance and fast charge kinetics is crucial to photocatalytic CO2 reduction into hydrocarbon fuels. Herein, we depict the interfacial Bi-O-C bond-bridged carbon quantum dot (CD)/oxygen vacancy-rich Bi2MoO6 (VOR-BMO) Ohmic junction photocatalysts. They were constructed by in-situ anchoring carbon quantum dots (CDs) onto the VOR-BMO hierarchical petal-like structures (HPs), which are self-assembled from numerous nanosheet subunits. Experimental analysis together with density functional theory (DFT) calculations indicate that the Bi-O-C bonds can provide the atomic-level interfacial channels, and introducing rich oxygen vacancies (VOs) into the BMO HPs can induce the enhanced built-in electric field (IEF) of the Ohmic heterojunction formed between VOR-BMO HPs and CDs, synergistically contributing the eminent charge transfer kinetics, so as to sharply boost the separation and transfer of the photoinduced electrons of VOR-BMO HPs into CDs with a near-zero resistance; Besides, the CD/VOR-BMO heterostructures can not only change the endoergic rate-determining step of VO-poor (VOP)-BMO HPs (i.e., CO* desorption to form CO) to an exoergic reaction process, thus promoting the CO* desorption from the photocatalyst surface, but also decrease the overall activation energy barrier. Consequently, in the presence of H2O vapor with no sacrificial agent, the optimum photocatalyst (CD/VOR-BMO-1.2) exhibits the fantabulous performances of CO2 photoreduction into CO with the production rate and selectivity of 60.00 μmol·g−1·h−1 and near 100 %, respectively. Such performances are comparable with the most recently reported photocatalysts for CO2 conversion. The study offers a valid strategy that harnesses the synergistic effect of the interfacial chemical bonds and Vo-induced enhanced IEF to design the high-efficiency photocatalysts for both energy and environmental applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recent decades, the exploitation and consumption of fossil fuels have resulted in a persistent elevation in atmospheric CO2 concentration [1,2]. The conversion of CO2 into hydrocarbon fuels can not only enhance the availability of viable energy resources, but also promote the emission and removal balance of global carbon [3,4]. Solar-energy driven conversion of CO2 and H2O into hydrocarbon fuels assisted from a photocatalyst is a promising strategy for achieving the above desired goal [5,6]. Since Halmann's pioneering report on photocatalytic CO2 conversion, numerous potential photocatalysts, including TiO2, CeO2, Zn2GeO4, C3N4, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [7–14], have been developed and extensively investigated. Recently, Bi2MoO6 (BMO), a representative Aurivillius phase oxide, has garnered significant attention in the field of photocatalytic CO2 conversion owing to its intrinsic chemical inertness, appropriate energy-band structure, and cost-effectiveness [15,16]. Such as, the BMO ultrathin nanosheets with the robust ferroelectricity achieve a good photocatalytic activity of CO2 reduction into CO (14.38 μmol·g−1·h−1) in the gas-solid system without sacrificial agents and co-catalysts [17]. However, the conversion efficiency of photocatalytic CO2 reduction over BMO is still unsatisfactory to pragmatic applications, primarily because of its sluggish charge kinetics, impeding the efficient use of photoinduced electrons and holes for CO2 reduction [17]. Therefore, it is of importance to develop the suitable BMO photocatalytic system with the outstanding charge-transfer kinetics.

Modifying BMO⁠ by cocatalysts is a feasible strategy to stimulate charge kinetics [18]. For instance, anchoring BMO nanosheets onto MXene (Ti3C2TX) nanosheets can lead to an upward band bending at both junction region until reaching the Fermi equilibrium, due to the higher work function (Φ) of Ti3C2TX nanosheets (6.13 eV) compared with that of BMO nanosheets⁠ (4.59 eV) [19]. The upward band bending will hinder the electrons in the conduction-band of the BMO nanosheets to inject into the Ti3C2TX nanosheets, signifying that injecting CB electrons of semiconductor into cocatalysts extremely depend on the Schottky-barrier height, when the Φ of semiconductor is lower compared to that of the cocatalyst [20]. Though the Schottky junction with the upward band bending can enhance the separation of charge carriers, transferring electrons from a semiconductor into cocatalysts requires an external force to overcome the Schottky barrier, lowering the photoinduced charge-carrier efficiency [20–22]. Encouragingly, Ohmic junction can be formed when the Φ of semiconductor is higher in contrast with that of the cocatalyst [23]. Thus, the electrons in the cocatalyst spontaneously migrate into the semiconductor until reaching the thermodynamic equilibrium, forming the downward band bending and a built-in electric field (IEF) with the direction from the cocatalyst to the semiconductor [24]. The Ohmic junction with the downward band bending has no potential barrier for the transfer of the photoinduced electrons in the semiconductor into the cocatalyst [25]. In this regard, the Ohmic junction is more effective for boosting the migration of photoinduced charge carriers, with respect to the Schottky junction [26]. At present，some Ohmic junction photocatalysts (such as, Ta3⁠N5⁠/Bi，BMO/Bi，ZnIn2S4/NiS, Cu@Cu2O) exhibit the improved photocatalytic performance of CO2 reduction [20,24,27,28]. However, the most of reported Ohmic junction photocatalysts suffer from the loose interface interaction, owing to the existence of weak physical adsorption and stack force between the two components [29]. This results in the serious charge-transfer resistance, restraining the trajection of charge flow. In addition, interfacial IEF can provide the driving force to boost the separation and transfer of photoinduced charge carriers, and thus the enhanced intensity of the interfacial IEF can lower the interfacial charge-transfer resistance [30]. Therefore, constructing the BMO-based Ohmic junction photocatalysts with the chemical bond level interface interaction and optimising IEF is expected to show the outstanding activity of CO2 photoreduction, yet this is rarely reported.

Given that CDs have unique properties: its lower Φ compared to that of BMO, ultra-small size, superior ability of light absorption, excellent electrical conductivity, and nontoxicity. Herein, CDs as the cocatalyst were in-situ anchored onto the surface of the nanosheet-subunit self-assembled BMO HPs with rich VOs to construct the interfacial Bi-O-C bond-bridged CD/VOR-BMO Ohmic junction photocatalyst. Experimental analysis combined with (DFT) calculations confirm that the interfacial chemical bonds (Bi-O-C) exist in the CD/VOR-BMO system, and the CDs and VOR-BMO HPs are the reaction sites for CO2 reduction and H2O oxidation, respectively. The interfacial Bi-O-C bonds can provide the atomic-level interfacial channels. Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM) ascertains that the introduction of rich VOs in the BMO HPs can induce the enhanced interfacial IEF of the Ohmic junction. The two merits can contribute the fast charge transfer kinetics, so as to sharply accelerate the separation and transfer of photoinduced electrons from VOR-BMO HPs into CDs (proved via the in-situ irradiated XPS) with the near-zero resistance. Additionally, the CD/VOR-BMO heterostructures can transform the endoergic rate-determining step of VOP-BMO HPs (i.e., CO* desorption to form CO) to an exoergic reaction process, which facilitates the CO* desorption from the photocatalyst surface, and lower the overall activation energy barrier. In consequence, in the presence of water vapor without any sacrificial agents, the optimized photocatalyst (CD/VOR-BMO-1.2) shows the prominent conversion efficiency of CO2 photoreduction into CO. The production rate of CO with the near 100 % of selectivity reaches up to 60.00 μmol·g−1·h−1, ca. 3.3 times as high as that of VOP-BMO HPs.

As shown in Scheme 1, VOR-BMO HPs were fabricated via a mild solvothermal means. Typically, 2 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5 H2O and 0.061 g of CTAB were dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water to obtain I solution. 1 mmol of Na2MoO4·2 H2O were dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water to obtain II solution, then the II solution was dropwise added into I solution under vigorous stirring. After 30 min of stirring, the 50 mL of final solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 120 ℃ for 24 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the as-prepared faint yellow powders were separated by centrifugation and washed with water for several times, and dried in a freeze dryer overnight. Furthermore, VOP-BMO HPs were obtained by the thermal treatment of VOR-BMO HPs under an air atmosphere at 200 ℃ for 8 h.

CDs were fabricated by an easy route of a bottom-up synthesis. Generally, 5.25 g of citric acid (C6H8O7) was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water, followed by adding 1.68 mL of ethylenediamine (EDA). After being stirred vigorously for 15 min at room temperature, the resultant mixture was transferred to a stainless-steel Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 12 h. After naturally being cooled to ambient temperature, the resulting brown black crude product underwent dialysis (M W∼1000 Da) to eliminate impurities. Finally, the purified CD solution was dried in a freeze dryer overnight to acquire CD solids.

CD/VOR-BMO hybrids were fabricated via a mild solvothermal means. Typically, 2 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5 H2O and 0.061 g of CTAB were dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water to obtain I solution. 1 mmol of Na2MoO4·2 H2O and a certain amount of CDs (0.0036, 0.0073 and 0.0109 g) were dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water to obtain II solution, then the II solution was dropwise added into I solution under vigorous stirring. After 30 min of stirring, the 50 mL of final solution was transferred into a 100 mL of Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 120 ℃ for 24 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the as-prepared faint yellow powders were separated by centrifugation and washed with water for several times, and dried in a freeze dryer overnight. The calculated weight ratios (R) of CDs to VOR-BMO HPs for the as-acquired samples are 0.6, 1.2 and 1.8 %. The corresponding samples are named as CD/VOR-BMO-0.6, CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 and CD/VOR-BMO-1.8, respectively. Additionally, CD/VOP-BMO-1.2 were obtained by the thermal treatment of CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 under an air atmosphere at 200 ℃ for 8 h.

The 0.0073 g of CDs and 0.3 g of as-obtained VOR-BMO HPs were mixed and transferred into an agate mortar. After strong grinding for 30 min, the uniform faint yellow powder was obtained, which was labelled as CD/VOR-BMO-M with R of 1.2 %.
The relevant materials, characterizations, and experimental methods are offered in the Supporting Information.

Fig. 1 displays the XRD patterns of pre-obtained samples. For CDs, a broad peak centred at ca. 27.3° can be observed, deriving from the (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [31]. As regards VOP-BMO HPs and VOR-BMO HPs, there are six peaks at 2θ values of 10.9, 28.3, 32.6, 36.1, 46.7 and 55.6◦, which are attributed to (020), (131), (002), (151), (202) and (133) crystal planes of BMO orthorhombic phase (JCPDS no. 21–0102), respectively. Besides, we can’t find the characteristic diffraction peaks of other phases or impurities, confirming that VOP-BMO HPs and VOR-BMO HPs are pure phase BMO, and that introducing rich VOs into BMO can’t affect its crystal structure. As for CD/BMO hybrids, they reserve the main diffraction peaks of BMO, whereas the peaks derived from CDs can’t detected, mainly owing to the fact that CDs on the surface of BMO are tiny size, small amount, high dispersion and disordered [32].

The FE-SEM images of VOR-BMO HPs (Fig. S1a,b) show the hierarchical petal-like structure with a rough surface. Its size is up to several micrometers, and ca. 30 nm for its thickness. The TEM image (Fig. S1c) further manifests that the hierarchical petal-like structure is self-assembled from lots of nanosheet subunits. Fig. S1d displays an obvious lattice spacing of 0.275 nm, being ascribed to the (200) lattice facet of BMO. Moreover, the blurry lattice fringes (labelled by white circle in Fig. S1d) evince the presence of VOs. As displayed in Fig. S2a,b, CDs exhibit a spherical structure with a main size distribution of 3.19±0.76 nm (Fig. S2d) and they are good dispersion without any obvious aggregation. The crystal lattice spacing of 0.321 nm for CDs is perfectly indexed to the (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon (Fig. S2c). After the introduction of CDs, CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 still keeps the hierarchical petal-like structure ( Fig. 2a,b). From its TEM image (Fig. 2c), we can find that numerous CDs (marked by yellow circles) are attached on the surfaces of BMO nanosheets. As shown in Fig. 2d, two sets of lattice fringes with the spacings of 0.275 and 0.321 nm correspond with the (200) plane of BMO and the (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon, respectively, and the lattice defects (labelled by white circles) can be found on CD/VOR-BMO-1.2, further affirming the existence of the VOs. Moreover, there is an obvious heterointerface along the white-dotted line, which confirms that CDs are tightly anchored on the surface, availing the sharply interfacial charge transfer (Fig. 2d). The EDX elemental mappings of CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 (Fig. 2e) indicate that it consists of the four elements of Bi (orange), Mo (blue), O (red) and C (turquoise), and that CDs are uniformly distributed onto the surface of BMO. Thermogravimetric analysis (Fig. S3) manifests that the sample of CDs exhibits ca. 11 % weight loss at the temperature ranging from 20 to 230 °C, because the adsorbed water on the surface of the catalysts is desorbed. Both CDs and CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 show a good thermal stability ranging from 0 to 230°C. Therefore, the thermal treatment of CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 under an air atmosphere at 200 ℃ can’t cause the decompose of CDs on its surface to obtain CD/VOP-BMO-1.2. Figs. S4,5 ascertain that VOP-BMO HPs and CD/VOP-BMO-1.2 inherit the hierarchical petal-like structure.

VOP-BMO HPs, VOR-BMO HPs, CD/VOP-BMO-1.2 and CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 exhibit the electron spin resonance (ESR) signals at a g-value of 2.003, resulting from the electrons ensnared on oxygen defects ( Fig. 3a) [33]. The strong intensities of the ESR signals for VOR-BMO HPs and CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 indicate that rich VOs exist in the two samples, and those of VOP-BMO HPs and CD/VOP-BMO-1.2 are obvious weaker, proving the existence of poor VOs in them. The diagram of the calculated density of states (DOS) for VOR-BMO (Fig. 3b) shows that its CB mainly consists of Bi, Mo and O elements, indicating that an interfacial Bi-O-C bond-bridged CD/BMO heterojunction can be established. Fig. 3c further demonstrates that the plentiful interfacial Bi-O-C bonds exist in the optimal configuration of CD/BMO heterojunction. Moreover, Fig. 3d presents the FT-IR patterns of the five pre-obtained samples, and the characteristic peaks at 3050–3660, 1635 and 1382 cm−1 are credited to the O-H of the adsorbed H2O on the sample surfaces [4]. For CDs, the observed peak at 1655 cm−1 is identified as the bending vibration of CO [34]. As regards VOP-BMO HPs and VOR-BMO HPs, the two bands at 444 and 581 cm−1 can be appointed to the stretching and deformation modes of Bi-O bonds, and the peaks located at 730 and 842 cm−1 can be contributed to the Mo-O stretching vibration.4 As for CD/VOP-BMO-1.2 and CD/VOR-BMO-1.2, the representative peaks for CDs (1655 cm−1) and those of the (VOP or VOR)-BMO HPs (444, 581, 730 and 842 cm−1) can be found, confirming that CDs and BMO coexist in the two samples. Besides, intriguingly, a new peak at ca. 938 cm−1 proves the formation of the interfacial Bi-O-C bonds in the above two samples [35]. In addition, the survey spectrum of CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 indicates that it contains the four elements of Bi, Mo, O and C (Fig. S6). As shown in Fig. 3e, for VOR-BMO HPs, the two peaks at 163.6 and 158.3 eV are contributed to Bi 4 f5/2 and Bi 4 f7/2 states of BMO with the reference values for Bi3+, respectively [4]. Compared to the corresponding peaks of VOP-BMO HPs, the double negatively shift by 0.1 eV, respectively, due to the existence of rich VOs in VOR-BMO HPs [36]. Besides, the two corresponding peaks of CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 slightly decrease to 163.5 and 158.2 eV, respectively. Noteworthily, for CD/VOR-BMO-1.2, two emerging peaks at 164.9 and 159.9 eV can be found, confirming the formation of interfacial Bi-O-C bonds between VOR-BMO HPs and CDs [37]. In Fig. 3f, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Mo 3d for VOR-BMO HPs, exhibits two peaks at 234.5 and 231.4 eV, which are attributed to Mo 3d3/2 and Mo 3d5/2 of BMO respectively [4]. The double peaks negatively shift by 0.1 eV, compared with those of VOP-BMO HPs, respectively, and the two relevant peaks of CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 further negatively shift by 0.1 eV, relative to those of VOR-BMO HPs, respectively. In Fig. 3g, as for VOP-BMO HPs, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1 s can be decomposed into the three peaks at 529.7, 531.3 and 532.2 eV, being ascribed to the crystal lattice oxygen, VOs and the adsorbed -OH groups from H2O, respectively [36]. The two peaks corresponding to the crystal lattice oxygen and Vo for CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 slight decrease to 529.6 and 531.0 eV, respectively. Interestingly, the area of peak credited to the VOs for VOR-BMO HPs is obviously larger than that of VOP-BMO HPs, indicating that VOR-BMO HPs exist in rich VOs, and poor VOs in VOP-BMO HPs. As regards CD/VOR-BMO-1.2, compared with VOR-BMO HPs, the peak derived from the crystal lattice oxygen negatively shift to 529.5 eV, and the two peaks resulting from the VOs and the adsorbed -OH groups from H2O don’t shift. Besides, a new peak at 533.2 eV can be assigned to the formed interfacial Bi-O-C bonds [37]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s for VOR-BMO@CD-1.2 (Fig. 3h) can be resolved into the four peaks at 284.8, 286.1, 287.2 and 588.9, which are assigned to C-C/CC, C-O, Bi-O-C, and CO bonds, respectively [37], indicating that CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 inherit the oxygen-containing groups (-OH and CO groups) derived from the CDs. The existence of these groups can boost the formation of atom-level heterointerface bridged by interfacial bonds. Meanwhile, the binding energies of the Bi 4 f (Bi3+), Mo 3d and O 1 s (the crystal lattice oxygen) in CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 are slightly down-shifted compared to those in VOR-BMO HPs (Fig. 3c,d,e), respectively, validating that the electrons in CDs spontaneously transfer into VOR-BMO HPs, because of the formation of the interfacial Bi-O-C bonds [4]. In addition, as displayed in Fig. 3i and Fig. S7, the charge accumulation and loss are represented by the yellow and cyan colours, respectively, also proving that in the CD/VOR-BMO system, the electrons in CDs can spontaneously move into VOR-BMO HPs. Bader charge analysis demonstrates that 2.91 e- directly migrates to VOR-BMO HPs from CDs in the CD/VOR-BMO system. Based on these aforesaid results, the interfacial Bi-O-C bond-bridged CD/VOR-BMO heterostructure has been prepared, not only providing the atomic-level interfacial channels so as to accelerate the fast migration of photo-generated electrons, but also guaranteeing the structural stability of CD/VOR-BMO-1.2.

The photocatalytic performances of CO2⁠ reduction over the seven pre-obtained samples were assessed in the CO2⁠ and water vapor system without any sacrificial agents. As displayed in Fig. 4a, under light irradiation for 4 h, all the pre-obtained samples ⁠present the photocatalytic activity of CO2⁠ reduction and CO is as the only product (Fig. 4a). Specifically, VOP-BMO HPs shows a weak total CO⁠ generation (73.28 μmol g−1), indicating that it alone cannot serve as a good photocatalyst, due to the rapid recombination of photoinduced carriers. The total yield of CO over VOR-BMO HPs is 130.30 μmol g−1, which is obviously improved compared to that of VOP-BMO HPs, owing to the introduction of rich VOs into VOP-BMO HPs. After anchoring CDs, the photocatalytic activities of the CD/VOR-BMO hybrids are gradually improved with the increase of the R values. The total CO yields of CD/VOP-BMO-0.6 and CD/VOP-BMO-1.8 are enhanced to 203.32 and 151.16 μmol·g−1, respectively, and the corresponding average CO generation rates are 50.92 and 37.75 μmol·g−1·h−1, respectively (Fig. 4a,b). The optimal photocatalyst (CD/VOR-BMO-1.2) exhibits the remarkable total CO⁠ generation (240.00 μmol g−1), and the corresponding average rate of CO evolution reaches up to 60.00 μmol g−1h−1, which is ca. 3.3 times as high as that of VOP-BMO HPs (Fig. 4a,b). Such a performance surpasses the most recently reported photocatalysts towards CO2 reduction (Table S1). The apparent quantum efficiency over CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 is evaluated as 1.72 % under the monochromatic LED light (385 nm) irradiation with the corresponding light intensity of ca. 20.0 mW·cm–2. Noteworthily, as displayed in Fig. S8, the intensity of the ESR signals at g=2.003 for the used CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 is slightly weaker that of the fresh CD/VOR-BMO-1.2, indicating that the concentration of VOs in the used CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 is slightly lower than that of the fresh CD/VOR-BMO-1.2. Therefore, for the above some samples, after the irradiation for 2 h, the CO yield slopes become gradually decreased with the prolongation of the irradiation time (Fig. 4a), mainly because some oxygen vacancies are filled, owing to the accumulation of the reactant intermediates on the surface of the photocatalysts, resulting in the poisoning of the photocatalysts [38,39]. Amazingly, all the CD/VOR-BMO hybrids show obviously enhanced photocatalytic activities in contrast to VOR-BMO HPs, CD/VOP-BMO-1.2 and CD/VOR-BMO-M, proving that the presence of rich VOs, CDs and interfacial Bi-O-C bonds can markedly boost the activity of CO2 photoreduction (Fig. 4a,b). After four continuous recycling tests of photocatalytic CO reduction under the identical experimental conditions, CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 still keeps 95.3 % of its reactivity (Fig. 4c). Besides, from the XRD patterns (Fig. S9a) and SEM images (Fig. 2a,b and Fig. S9b) of CD/BMO-1.2 before and after the recycling tests of CO2 photoreduction, no obvious changes can be found. These demonstrate the good stability of CD/VOR-BMO-1.2, fitting the repeated application. As presented in Fig.S10, the control tests (under either no irradiation or no photocatalysts or using N2 to replace CO2) clarify that there is no appreciable CO production being perceived. Moreover, the weak peak with the retention time of around 10.930 min in the GC trace (Fig. S11) can be assigned to the CO product. The corresponding mass spectra (Fig. 4d) exhibits a relatively high intensity of m/z = 29 (13CO) along with the detected fragments of m/z = 13 (13C) and m/z = 16 (O). These results validate that the generated CO stems from the CO2 reduction with the help of the photocatalyst and light illumination, rather than the residual carbons on the surface of the photocatalyst. Besides, by the 13CO2 isotope labeling experiment over CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 (Fig. S11), CH4, C2H4, C2H6, CH3OH products cannot be detected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced mechanism of photocatalytic CO2 reduction
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the optical properties of the representative pre-obtained samples were investigated via UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS). As shown in Fig. S12a, the CDs show the wide light absorption range from 200 nm to 800 nm, due to the good light harvesting ability of the narrow gap sp2 carbon clusters in the CDs. Both VOP-BMO HPs and VOR-BMO HPs display the good capability of UV–visible light absorption with the absorption edges of ca. 465 and 470 nm, respectively, and their corresponding band gap energies are estimated as ca. 2.80 and 2.77 eV, respectively (Fig. S12b). Notably, compared with VOP-BMO HPs, VOR-BMO HPs exhibit the obviously enhanced ability of light absorption at the range from 500 nm to 800 nm, owing to the rich VOs existing in VOR-BMO HPs. Besides, after coupling with CDs, the light absorption abilities (500–800 nm) of CD/VOP-BMO-1.2 and CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 are stronger than these of VOP-BMO HPs and VOR-BMO HPs, respectively. The conduction band (CB) values of VOP-BMO-PMs and VOR-BMO-HPs are evaluated by Mott-Schottky curves. Fig. S12c,d exhibit a positive slope, indicating that VOP-BMO HPs and VOR-BMO HPs are the n-type semiconductors. Given that the CB value of the n-type semiconductor is close to its flat band potential, the CB values of the VOP-BMO HPs and VOR-BMO HPs can be assessed as −0.46 and −0.50 V vs RHE, respectively. Distinctly, their CB positions (ECB) are more negative than that of CO/CO2 (-0.12 V vs RHE), manifesting their high reduction capability to drive the CO2 photoreduction. In addition, the valence band (VB) positions (EVB) of VOP-BMO HPs and VOR-BMO HPs are evaluated as 2.34 and 2.27 V vs RHE according to the band gap equation (Eg = EVB - ECB). The calculated work function (Φ) of VOP-BMO-HPs, VOR-BMO-HPs and CDs is 6.47, 6.35 and 5.27 eV (Fig. S13a-c). Fig. S14 summarizes the band structures of VOP-BMO HPs, VOR-BMO HPs and CDs. Clearly, the Fermi level (EF) of CDs is lower than that of VOP-BMO HPs and VOR-BMO HPs before the coupling of (VoP or VoR)-BMO HPs and CDs. After coupling BMO-HPs with CDs, electrons in CDs will transfer spontaneously into VOP-BMO HPs or VOR-BMO HPs until their EFs near the heterointerface are equal. The band edge of VOP-BMO HPs or VOR-BMO HPs is downward bending so as to form a Ohmic heterojunction and built an internal built-in electric field (IEF) with the direction from CDs to BMO HPs near their interface ( Fig. 5a). The Ohmic heterojunction can restrain the migration of photogenerated electrons from CDs to BMO HPs due to the downward band bending of BMO HPs. Under light irradiation, the IEF can drive the transfer of photogenerated electrons from the CB of BMO HPs to CDs (Fig. 5b). To affirm this viewpoint, the in-situ irradiated XPS tests were performed under visible-light irradiation. From Fig. 6a–d, we can find that the binding energies of Bi 4 f, Mo 3d and O 1 s in CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 under irradiation positively shift in contrast with those in CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 under dark, and that the binding energy of C 1 s in the hybrid under irradiation negatively shift in comparison to that in the hybrid under dark. The in-situ irradiated XPS results validate that the electrons in the BMO HPs move into CDs under irradiation. More importantly, the EF of VOR-BMO HPs is different from that of VOP-BMO HPs, indicating that the Vo engineering can be a feasible strategy to adjust the IEF of the Ohmic heterojunction so as to facilitate the transfer and separation of photogenerated charge carriers (Fig. 5c).

To prove the above standpoint, Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM) was employed to investigate the surface potential and the migration and separation behavior of photogenerated carriers over CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 and CD/VOP-BMO-1.2. As shown in Fig. S15a and c, the surface potential of CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 in the dark is 67.0 mV, which is obviously higher than that of CD/VOP-BMO-1.2 (7.9 mV) (Fig. S15b and d). This confirms that CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 exhibits distinctly stronger IEF than that of CD/VOP-BMO-1.2 [40]. Both CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 and CD/VOP-BMO-1.2 exhibit SPV response under light irradiation (Fig. S15a′ and b′), deriving from the migration of photoinduced electrons from the (VoP or VOR)-BMO HPs to CDs. The higher concentration of photoinduced electrons before and after light illumination can be directly reflected by the more negative surface photovoltage spectroscopy (SPV) signal change [41]. Fig. 7a, b, a′ and b′ manifest the obviously improved electron concentration gathering on the surface of CDs in the CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 than on that of CDs in the CD/VOP-BMO-1.2. As displayed in Fig. 7c,d, the surface photovoltage change by subtracting the potential under dark conditions from that under illumination is labeled as ΔCPD. The value of ΔCPD for CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 is computed as 39.7 mV, higher that CD/VOP-BMO-1.2 of 15.0 mV, confirming that the obviously enhanced charge separation efficiency due to the stronger IEF in the heterointerface of CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 [40,41]. Therefore, the combination of the interfacial Bi-O-C bonds and the Vo-induced enhanced IEF of Ohmic junction can endow CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 with the extremely fast photoinduced electron migration from VOR-BMO HPs to CDs, approaching a zero-resistance transfer [42]. In addition, we acquire the transient photocurrent-time curves of the pre-obtained samples. They were measured under chopped light illumination (λ > 420 nm) with the five off-on switching cycles. As depicted in Fig. 7e, VOP-BMO HPs manifests the poorest photocurrent intensity, because of the rapid recombination of photoinduced carriers, whereas that of VOR-BMO HPs is obviously improved, because that the introduced rich VOs can serve as the electron traps so as to enhance the separation rate of photoinduced charge carriers. After loading the CDs, the photocurrent intensity progressively decreases in the following order: CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 > VOR-BMO HPs > CD/VOP-BMO-1.2 > VOP-BMO HPs> CD/VOR-BMO-M, clarifying the stronger IEF and Bi-O-C bonds in the heterointerface of CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 can synergistically boost the transfer and separation of the photoinduced electron-hole pairs. The smallest semicircle for CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 (Fig. 7f) validate that it features the lowest resistance and fastest rate of charge migration from the bulk to the surface, which is most conducive to the separation and migration of photoinduced carriers. From Fig. 7g, we can observe that the average fluorescence lifetimes of different samples are arranged as follows: CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 (1.23 ns) > VOR-BMO HPs (1.22 ns) > CD/VOP-BMO-1.2(1.20 ns) > VOP-BMO HPs (1.17 ns). This signifies that the fast charge transfer for CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 leads to the more photoinduced carriers for taking part in the reaction of photocatalytic CO2 reduction. In addition, the adsorption energy (E ads) after H2O or CO2 adsorption over different surfaces of CD/VoR-BMO were computed ( Fig. 8). As exhibited in Fig. 8a,b, the E ads of CO2 over BMO (010) is −0.17 eV, lower than that of CDs (-0.30 eV), respectively. The E ads of H2O over BMO (010) is −1.1 eV, higher than that of CDs (-0.58 eV), respectively (Fig. 8c,d). These results suggest that for the CD/VOR-BMO system, CDs and VOR-BMO HPs serve as the reaction sites for CO2 reduction and H2O oxidation, respectively [43].

CO2 adsorption and activation on the surface of catalysts are crucial to the reaction of photocatalytic CO2 reduction. Commonly, a photocatalyst has a larger specific surface area (S BET), and thus can provide more active sites, which avail the adsorption and activation of reactants, promoting the kinetics of surface photocatalytic reaction. As shown in Fig. S16, based on the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller model, the S BET of VOR-BMO HPs was calculatetd as 33.18 m2·g−1, higher that of VOP-BMO HPs (11.51 m2·g−1), due to the introduction of rich VOs in the VOR-BMO HPs, benefiting CO2 photoreduction. However, the S BET of CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 (30.02 m2·g−1) becomes smaller than that of VOR-BMO HPs, because the loading CDs on the surface of VOR-BMO HPs have a small S BET (2.15 m2·g−1). This excludes the reason that the enhanced photocatalytic activity of CD/VOR-BMO-1.2, compared with that of VOR-BMO HPs, is derived from its improved S BET. In addition, the CO2-TPD and CO-TPD curves of the pre-obtained samples were obtained for investigating their performances of CO2 adsorption and CO desorption. As displayed in Fig. 9a, for VOP-BMO HPs, there are two desorption peaks at 329.8 and 561.1 °C, which are labeled as peak I and peak II, respectively. The two corresponding peaks of VOR-BMO HPs positively shift to 337.6 and 571.4 °C, and their peak areas are larger than those of VOP-BMO HPs, respectively. Besides, compared with VOR-BMO HPs, the peak I of CD/VOP-BMO-1.2 positively transfer to 345.5 °C and the area of its peak II obviously become larger. These results infer that CD/VOP-BMO-1.2 avail the CO2 adsorption due to the introduction of rich VOs and CDs. The CO-TPD curve of VOP-BMO HPs (Fig. 9b) shows two evident desorption peaks at 80–200 °C and 280–400°C, respectively, signifying that its strong physical adsorption and chemisorption of CO. Intriguingly, for CD/VOR-BMO-1.2, no obvious peak at 80–200 °C and a small peak at 280–400°C can be detected, clarifying that it is beneficial to the CO desorption from its surface (Fig. 9b). The intermediates formed on CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 during the process of photocatalytic CO2 reduction were surveyed via in-situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform (DRIFT) spectroscopy. Fig. 9c indicates that the intensities of multiple peaks gradually become stronger with the increase of the illumination time. Specifically, a small peak at 1652 cm−1 is identified as CO2 - [44]. Due to the co-permeation of CO2 and H2O, there are the typical peaks of bicarbonates (HCO3 -) at 1683, 1635, 1487 and 1474 cm−1), bidentate carbonate (b-CO3 2-) at 1506, 1339,1374 and 1360 cm−1), monodentate carbonate (m-CO3 2-) at 1454, 1319, and 1287 cm−1) being detected [45]. Significantly, the characteristic peaks of CO2*(1575 and 1257 cm−1) unveil that the CO2 adsorption and activation indeed happen on the photocatalyst surface [46]. Additionally, two apparent peaks at 1557 and 1541 cm−1, resulting from COOH*, affirm that COOH* is the intermediate for the CO2 photoreduction into CO [47]. According to the intermediates identified by the in situ DRIFT spectroscopy, the Gibbs free energies profile for CO2 reduction into CO on VOP-BMO HPS and CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 were simulated by DFT calculations. Fig. 9d demonstrates that for VOP-BMO HPS, the CO* desorption to form CO features the highest free-energy barrier (5.69 eV) and thus it is the rate-determining step, demonstrating that the CO2 reduction into CO on VOP-BMO HPS is thermodynamically difficult. Amazingly, as regards CD/VOR-BMO-1.2, it can transform the endoergic rate-determining step of VOP-BMO HPS into an exoergic reaction process, and the formed gas phase CO is with an exothermic by −0.44 eV. This suggests that CO* desorption from the surface of CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 is easy, which is also proved by CO-TPD. Moreover, it can also decrease the overall activation energy barrier because its rate-determining step (namely, the hydrogenation of CO2* to form COOH*) presents a low free-energy barrier of 0.75 eV. Therefore, these merits endow CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 with the preeminent photocatalytic CO2 into CO.

Integrating the results of the above experiments and DFT calculations, we propose a plausible improved photocatalytic mechanism for CD/VOR-BMO-1.2. As depicted in Fig. 10, after coupling VOR-BMO HPs with CDs, the electrons in CDs transfer into VOR-BMO HPs, leading to the downward bend of VOR-BMO HPs band edges until both Fermi levels at their heterointerface are uniform, owing to the smaller ΦCDs with respect to the ΦBMO. Simultaneously, the Ohmic junction along with the IEF directing from the CDs to the VOR-BMO HPs can be formed at the heterointerface. Upon simulated sunlight irradiation, the photoinduced electrons in the CB of VOR-BMO HPs excited from its VB, migrate into the CDs, due to the driving of the IEF, thus leaving the photoinduced holes in the VB of VOR-BMO HPs. The formed interfacial Bi-O-C bonds lead to the atom-level interfacial channels, and introducing the rich VOs can induce the obviously enhanced IEF. The two merits endow CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 with the ultrafast photoinduced-electron transfer of a near-zero resistance from the VOR-BMO HPs to CDs. In addition, CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 can convert the endoergic rate-determining step of VOP-BMO HPs (namely, CO* desorption to form CO) to an exoergic reaction process so as to boost CO* desorption from the photocatalyst surface, and decrease the overall activation energy barrier. As a consequence, CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 can exhibit the remarkable photocatalytic performance of CO2 reduction into CO.

In conclusion, a serious of interfacial Bi-O-C bond-bridged CD/VOR-BMO hierarchical heterostructures have been fabricated via the in-situ anchoring of CDs onto the nanosheet-subunit self-assembled VOR-BMO hierarchical petal-like structures. DFT calculations demonstrate that for the CD/VOR-BMO system, CDs and VOR-BMO HPs are the reaction sites for CO2 reduction as well as H2O oxidation, respectively. Upon simulated sunlight illumination with no sacrificial reagent, the optimum photocatalyst (CD/VOR-BMO-1.2) manifests the prominent CO2 photoreduction into CO, and its activity and selectivity are 60.00 μmol g−1h−1 and near 100 %, respectively. This is mainly attributed to the following synergy: (1) the interfacial Bi-O-C bonds in the CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 can provide the atomic-level interfacial channels, and the rich VOs in the hybrid can induce the improved IEF of the Ohmic junction, synergistically accelerating the photoinduced electron transfer of a near-zero resistance from VOR-BMO HPs to CDs; (2) CD/VOR-BMO-1.2 can not only change the endoergic rate-determining step of VOP-BMO HPs (CO* desorption to form CO) to an exoergic reaction process, promoting CO* desorption from the surface of the photocatalyst, but also decrease the overall activation energy barrier. This work contributes a valid strategy for designing the excellent photocatalysts to both energy and environmental applications by using the synergistic effect of the interfacial chemical bonds and Vo-induced enhanced IEF.
